figures.caption: Table 1: Comprehensive evaluation of multivariate forecasting performance across diverse real-world domains. AVG refers to the overall average across all horizons. The best and second-best performances are highlighted. Full results can be found in Table 14.

Figure 12: Visualization of forecasting cases from Weather by different models under the input-96predict-60 setting. Notably, for LeapTS, both the background regions and the predicted line segments are highlighted with various colors to explicitly illustrate the distinct dynamic scheduling segments.

Figure 11: Visualization of forecasting cases from PEMS04 by different models under the input-96predict-48 setting. Notably, for LeapTS, both the background regions and the predicted line segments are highlighted with various colors to explicitly illustrate the distinct dynamic scheduling segments.

Table 9: Ablation study on the hierarchical scheduling mechanism. We compare the full LeapTS with a variant (w/o High-Level) that directly predicts the step length without the high-level scale selection. The forecasting horizon is set to P = 36 for ILI and P = 60 for others. Average denotes the mean performance. The best performances are highlighted.
Table 10: Sensitivity analysis of the number of hidden layers on ETTm1 dataset (L = 96). Average denotes the mean performance across all prediction lengths P .

Figure 1: Comparison of multi-horizon forecasting paradigms. (a) The Direct paradigm predicts the full horizon in a single forward pass but is temporally decoupled. (b) The Recursive paradigm forecasts step by step but suffers from error accumulation. (c) Our paradigm formulates multi-horizon forecasting as a closed-loop adaptive scheduling process, enabling flexibility and temporal coherence.

Table 3: Full forecasting results on three synthetic datasets: (1) high-dimensional heterogeneous series, (2) causally driven series, and (3) univariate series with latent dynamics. Baselines are optimized over an wider hyperparameter search space (see Appendix C). AVG refers to the average across all prediction lengths. The best and second-best performances are highlighted.
Figure 3: t-SNE visualization in Scenario 1. LeapTS successfully distinguishes the variable clusters, whereas baselines fail.
Table 11: Sensitivity analysis of input look-back lengths (L) on ETTm1 dataset. Average denotes the mean performance across all prediction lengths P .
Table 13: Error bars for LeapTS on six real-world datasets across 90%, 95%, and 99% confidence levels.
Table 12: Sensitivity analysis of the number of variable clusters (G) on the ETTm1 dataset. Average denotes the mean performance across all prediction lengths P .

Figure 8: Visualization of forecasting behavior and latent state evolution. The top row shows the predictions across three synthetic scenarios, where different background colors indicate distinct scheduling segments. The bottom rows display the corresponding evolution of the hidden states across successive scheduling stages.
Figure 7: Validation loss on the ETTm1 and Weather datasets. Under the same hyperparameter settings, LeapTS converges faster and reaches a lower final loss.
Table 16: Long-term forecasting results on representative datasets. The input sequence length is set to 720 for all models. The best and second-best results are highlighted.

Table 15: Full univariate forecasting results on the M4 Competition dataset [Makridakis et al., 2018] across different frequencies. Average denotes the weighted average across frequency groups following the standard M4 evaluation. The best and second-best performances are highlighted.
Figure 4: Visualization of forecasting behavior and latentstate evolution across three synthetic scenarios.
Figure 5: Scheduling behaviors on synthetic data. (a) Category Distribution: proportions across volatility levels. (b) Scheduling Intensity: average scheduling count.

Figure 2: Framework of LeapTS. (a) LeapTS reformulates multi-horizon forecasting as an adaptive scheduling problem. The scheduling branch progressively generates fine-grained forecasts and latent states evolve through variate-specific controlled dynamics. (b) The H-Controller performs scale selection then advancement-length selection. The generated segment is written into the target horizon, and the controller state updates based on current scheduling progress for subsequent steps.
Table 7: Summary of datasets used in this work. We include both real-world and synthetic datasets across multivariate and univariate forecasting tasks.

Table 14: Full multivariate forecasting results on real-world datasets. The input sequence length is set to 36 for the ILI dataset and 96 for all other datasets. AVG refers to the overall average across all horizons. The best and second-best performances are highlighted.

Figure 9: Visualization of forecasting cases from Electricity by different models under the input-96predict-60 setting. Notably, for LeapTS, both the background regions and the predicted line segments are highlighted with various colors to explicitly illustrate the distinct dynamic scheduling segments.

Figure 10: Visualization of forecasting cases from ETTh1 by different models under the input-96predict-60 setting. Notably, for LeapTS, both the background regions and the predicted line segments are highlighted with various colors to explicitly illustrate the distinct dynamic scheduling segments.
Figure 6: Training and inference efficiency on ETTh2 and Weather. Bubble size indicates memory (training) or parameter size (inference).
